directions.name: Trim The Remaining PTX Export Scoreboard
directions.hypothesis: The zero-padding export trim already recovered runtime, so the remaining gap is likely in the PTX export lifetime itself: the scratch row-pair path still carries enough address indirection and shared-memory residence to raise long-scoreboard stalls. Narrowing that export path further should lower scoreboard cost without reintroducing the DRAM or barrier inflation seen in the failed families.
directions.expected_bottleneck: PTX export-lifetime latency, shared scratch residency, and address-generation overhead in the hot-band store path.
directions.risk: Low to medium. The change surface is narrow and still inside the improved restored PTX baseline, but the long-scoreboard bump may already be the cheapest remaining part of the design.
directions.stop_condition: Keep this direction only if it cuts long-scoreboard stalls and beats 25.64300728 ms; otherwise close it and move to a sibling feed/orchestration family.

directions.name: One More Bounded 128x128 Two-Stage Feed Iteration
directions.hypothesis: The 128x128 two-stage sibling is still the only alternate family that has already shown a real improvement path. A bounded cp.async / stage-handoff tweak may recover the remaining runtime while keeping the DRAM footprint low and avoiding the closed K32 staging topology.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Copy-feed latency and stage handoff inside the 128x128 two-stage kernel, with long-scoreboard pressure rather than raw tensor under-utilization.
directions.risk: Medium. This family has already paid off once, but it may be near its local limit unless the remaining scoreboard comes from the feed cadence itself.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the next run fails to improve on the restored PTX baseline or if the stall mix simply shifts without reducing long-scoreboard cost.

directions.name: Tighten PTX Hot-Band Grouping And Peel Handoff
directions.hypothesis: The remaining cost may be coming from the PTX hot-band grouping and peeled-row boundary rather than the export itself. A narrow orchestration change around group sizing or peel launch could remove redundant coordination while staying within the low-risk PTX-adjacent surface.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Hot-band orchestration and peel coordination, especially around grouped-row mapping and the fixed tail handoff.
directions.risk: Medium to high. This is a bounded change, but it is farther from the proven export trim and could fail to move the long-scoreboard bottleneck.
directions.stop_condition: Abandon this family if the profile stays effectively unchanged or if the tweak pushes the kernel back toward the closed default-promotion behavior.

notes: Round 81/100 diagnosis for run 20260420_115626_bf16_gemm_v1_469a12b. The zero-padding PTX export trim improved runtime to 25.64300728 ms, with tensor activity back at 48.00 and barrier down at 5.50, so the restored PTX baseline is now the current reference. The new concern is long-scoreboard pressure at 7.61, not DRAM or barrier inflation; the broad default-promotion family and the K32 staged family remain closed-negative, so the next work should stay in the PTX-adjacent export/feed/orchestration space rather than reopening those paths.
